title: Make the board talk back
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Upload a sketch with no wiring at all, open Serial Monitor, and watch the ESP32-S3 print a start-up line and then count its own running time in seconds. This is the day you gain a window into a running program — the one tool that turns every later "why isn't it working?" into a question you can actually answer by asking the board what it's doing.

sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

parts.name: Official sketch
parts.explanation: A sketch is the Arduino name for a program. This lesson uses Freenove's Sketch_19.1_Ultrasonic_Ranging.ino so you can focus first on wiring, observation, and the measurement model.

coachInstructions: Keep the day to upload-and-read; there is nothing to wire.
Frame serial as a window into a running program — the same USB bridge from Day 2, now carrying text from the board back to the screen.
If the monitor shows garbled text, explain the characters are arriving but read at the wrong rate; the fix is to set the monitor to 115200 to match the sketch.
If the monitor is blank, check board, port, and a data-capable cable, then have the learner press reset so setup() prints again.
For the challenge, help them add a counter and print it, then remove delay(1000) so they see how fast the board really loops — the point is that printing reveals a value they could not otherwise see.
Keep the Arduino path primary; MicroPython's print() lands in Thonny's shell and is optional only if already set up.
steps: Plug the ESP32-S3 into your computer with a data-capable USB cable.
In Arduino IDE, select your ESP32-S3 board and the port it appears on.
Press Upload and wait for the IDE to say Done uploading.
Open Serial Monitor and set the baud rate to 115200.
Watch the start-up line appear, then a running-time count that climbs every second.

arduino.explanation: Opens the USB serial link and sets its speed to 115200 baud — the same number Serial Monitor has to use, so both ends read the stream the same way.
Sends one line of text up the cable, then moves to the next line — a start-up marker that proves the link is live before any real data flows.
Slots a live value into a text template — one decimal place — and sends it, so a number inside the program becomes something you can read.

micropython.explanation: In MicroPython, print() sends text up the same USB serial link Thonny reads.
Milliseconds since the board started, divided into seconds — the same running clock as the Arduino sketch.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: The USB-to-serial bridge from Day 2 now runs the other way — text from the board to your screen — which is the only way to see a value the program holds but never shows.
theoryModel.formula: readable text needs board baud = monitor baud

notes.title: Why a wrong baud is garbage, not silence
notes.body: The characters still arrive on the wire. Baud sets how often each side reads the signal, so a mismatched monitor samples between the real bits and reconstructs the wrong letters. The fix is always to match the number, never to resend.

notes.title: Printing is how you debug from here on
notes.body: When a later sketch misbehaves, a print of the suspect value tells you what the code truly saw — a sensor reading, a state, a counter — instead of guessing from an LED. Reach for a print before you rewrite anything.

test.successCriteria: The first line prints once at start-up; the running time then climbs by about one each second.

firstChecks: The characters are arriving; the monitor is reading them at the wrong rate. Set Serial Monitor to 115200 to match the sketch.
Confirm the right board and port, then press the board's reset button so setup() runs and prints the start-up line again.

troubleshooting.symptom: Only the first line, no count
firstChecks: Give it a second — the running time prints once per delay(1000).
Swap in a data-capable USB cable.

challenge: Add a counter that the loop increments and prints, then remove the delay so you watch how fast the board truly runs — a number that was there all along but had no way to show itself.
